key_points: Health is influenced by biological, environmental, and social factors
Mental health and emotional well-being significantly affect physical health
Urbanization and pollution increase the risk of respiratory and infectious diseases
Communicable diseases spread through multiple transmission pathways including vectors, contaminated food, and airborne droplets
Lifestyle factors such as inactivity and poor diet contribute to non-communicable diseases
Vaccination programs help build community immunity and reduce disease outbreaks
Misuse of antibiotics leads to antibiotic resistance, a major global health problem
examples: Mosquito breeding in stagnant water increases malaria and dengue cases
Air pollution in cities increases respiratory diseases like asthma
Antibiotic misuse allows bacteria to evolve resistance
Community vaccination programs helped eradicate smallpox globally
scientific_concepts: Immunity: The immune system identifies pathogens and produces antibodies
Vaccination: Introduction of weakened pathogens to stimulate immune memory
Antibiotic Resistance: Bacteria evolve mechanisms to survive antibiotic treatment
Disease Transmission Cycle: Pathogen → Host → Mode of transmission → New host
prevention_strategies: Community sanitation and public health programs
Vector control through elimination of stagnant water
Responsible use of antibiotics prescribed by doctors
Mass immunization campaigns to control infectious diseases
Public awareness about hygiene and disease prevention
exam_tips: Explain differences between communicable and non-communicable diseases with examples
Understand cause-and-effect relationships between environment and disease spread
Study how vaccines stimulate immune response
Be able to explain antibiotic resistance with examples
Link lifestyle choices with long-term health outcomes
